original_abstract: While Thomas Jefferson famously asserted that all men are created equal, the historical treatment of migrants tells a far more complicated story. In this volume, twelve distinguished scholars analyze how nations—including France, the United States, Turkey, Canada, Mexico, the Netherlands, Sweden, and Denmark—have historically categorized and differentiated among migrants, as well as the rhetoric used to justify such distinctions in policy and public discourse. The chapters are organized into a triptych, exploring three central questions: who qualifies as a refugee, who constitutes a family, and how "difference" is defined. Collectively, these studies demonstrate that these categories are inextricably linked. They intersect in complex ways, shaped not only by geopolitics and economic shifts but also by the specific gender, class, ethnicity, religion, and sexual orientation of the migrants themselves.
humanized_abstract: Thomas Jefferson famously claimed that all men are created equal. In practice, this has never been true for migrants. In this new collection, twelve leading migration scholars show how states around the world have distinguished and legislated different categories of migrants (in France, the United States, Turkey, Canada, Mexico, the Netherlands, Sweden and Denmark, among other places). This book is constructed as a triptych with each panel corresponding to one of the three central questions in the migration field: who is a refugee? Who is a family? And what is difference? All three categories and their interconnections are explored through detailed ethnographic analyses that highlight the gender, class, ethnic, religious and sexuality dynamics at play, which are shaped by political economy and structural factors as much as by everyday social and cultural interactions.
